english: other synonymous phrases: _dod-lóg spyód-pa ('dod log spyod pa), mi _ós-pai spyód-pa byéd-pa (mi 'os pai spyod pa byed pa), Glr., nyál-po (nyal po), čágs-pa (chags pa), kríg-pa spyód-pa (khrig pa spyod pa), belong by their construction properly to 1; so also: bud-méd brgya spyod nus (bud med brgya spyod nus) he can get done with a hundred wives, Lt. II. sbst. 1. action, practice, execution, opp. to ltá-ba (lta ba), theory. esp. in mysticism, v. sgóm-pa (sgom pa). — 2. activity: spyód-pa šin-tu dóg-par gyúr-to (spyod pa shin tu dog par gyur to) they were much restrained, narrowly watched Glr.; séms-kyi spyód-pa (sems kyi spyod pa) seems to be: faculty of mind, Wdn. — 3. way of acting, conduct, course of life, = spyód-lam (spyod lam); byaṅ-čub-séms-dpai (byang chub sems dpai) frq; ṅán- (ngan) or nyés-spyod (nyes spyod) bad actions, bzáṅ- (bzang) or légs-spyod (legs spyod) good actions Cs.; spyód-pa žìb-pa (spyod pa zhib pa), 'the strict', a monastic order Pth.; behaviour, deportment, frq.: spyód-pa rtsìṅ-ba (spyod pa rtsing ba), rude, rough, in manners Glr.; spyód-pas skád-čig kyaṅ mi tsugs (spyod pas skad cig kyang mi tshugs), of an extremely variable conduct (lit. not for one moment the same) Glr. Comp. spyod-grós (spyod gros) gait and deportment Mil. — spyod-ṅán (spyod ngan) = ṅán-spyod (ngan spyod), spyod-ṅán byéd pa (spyod ngan byed pa). — spyód-tsul (spyod tshul), Sch. = spyód-pa II (spyod pa II). spyód-yul (spyod yul), sphere of activity; kún-gyi spyód-yul _di ma lags (kun gyi spyod yul 'di ma lags), that is not a thing to be attempted by every body Mil.; mtóṅ-bai spyód-pa (mthong bai spyod pa) range of vision Tar.; cf. गोचर. — spyód-lam (spyod lam), 1. demeanour, deportment, mode of life frq.; 2. good behaviour, graceful demeanour, noble deportment; otherwise spyód-pa mdzés-pa (spyod pa mdzes pa); hence spyód-pa daṅ ldán-pa (spyod pa dang ldan pa), spyod-ldán (spyod ldan) of genteel manners Dzl.; spyód-pa daṅ mi ldán-pa (spyod pa dang mi ldan pa) Dzl., *čod-ṅán-čan* (*cod ngan can*) W., *čǫ́-lóg jhé-kẹ́n* (*cǫ́ log jhe kẹn*), C. rude, unmannerly, ill-bred, disobedient. 3. Med.: diet, and more particularly bodily exercise; zas-spyód (zas spyod), food and exercise. 4. attitude: spyód-lam rnam-bži (spyod lam rnam bzhi) the four attitudes of sitting, lying, standing and walking.
or dpyod-pád (dpyod pad) (spelling not quite certain), pronunc.: *čǫ́-pe'* (*cǫ́ pe'*), lemon, citron C.

pf. spyoms (spyoms), to boast, to exhibit with ostentation, e.g. virtues, (the Greek καυχᾶσθαι). Notwithstanding the detailed explanations of the Lexx., the word is after all so little known, that I never met with it in books, nor heard it used by the people. — spyoms (spyoms), sbst., self-praise, boasting Zam.
monkey. Mil., prob. the large dark-gray, long-tailed monkey of the southern Himalaya; sprá-mo (spra mo); spra-prúg (spra phrug).
v. pra-čál (pra chal); spra-tél (spra tel) v. tél-pa (tel pa).

I. sbst. W. *šrá-wa* (*shra wa*), spunk, German tinder, prepared of the fibres of a thistle (Cousinia); spra-mé (spra me), glowing tinder, Pth.; pyi ni sprá-ba dkár-por gyogs (phyi ni spra ba dkar por gyogs), white-nappy, as a botanical term, Wdn., the colour of the tinder, referred to, being a light gray; sprá-bai tóg-gu (spra bai thog gu) a medicinal herb Wdn. II. vb. pf. spras (spras), imp. spros (spros), 1. to adorn, to decorate: rgyán-gyis (rgyan gyis) frq., mtsán-dpes (mtshan dpes) Mil. and elsewh. — 2. gčes spras (gces spras), Lex.? sprá-ba byéd-pa (spra ba byed pa) to love, to caress. — 3. perh. identical with *šrá-če(s)* (*shra ce(s)*), to empty (a dish). — 4. spra _khrid-pa (spra 'khrid pa) to lead, to direct right. — Cf. also gtsaṅ sprá-wa (gtsang spra ba).

Cs., to beg; (the verb I never met with, and Zam. explains the sbst. only by nor-méd (nor med)); spráṅ-po (sprang po), beggar, Dzl., Glr., frq. (Wts. 'filou', rather bold, though not far from the truth); *ṭaṅ-lóṅ* (*ṭang long*) C., id.; spraṅ-rgán (sprang rgan) Mil., an old beggar; rdzús-mai spráṅ-po (rdzus mai sprang po) a sham-beggar Glr.; spraṅ-prúg (sprang phrug) beggar boy; spraṅ-bán (sprang ban) mendicant friar Glr.; spraṅ-zás (sprang zas) beggar's livelihood Mil; dkar-spraṅ (dkar sprang) begging for lenten food, also such food obtained by begging, v. dkar-zás (dkar zas); skyur-spráṅ (skyur sprang) begging for beer Mil.
སི་ཅེན si-čén (si cen), n. of the emperor of China, during whose reign Buddhism was introduced into that country,
